answer:
**The work is *Traité de Législation: VOL I* by Charles Comte, and the relevant chapter is Chapter 21 ("De l’action des lois de la morale, et des obstacles que cette action rencontre quelquefois dans cell").**[^1]

This chapter closes Volume I. Near its end, the author describes two careers open to every person—one of virtues and one of vices/crimes—and states that the best safeguard for nations is to place over the entrance to the latter "this terrible inscription from Dante's hell":

> PER ME SI VA NELLA CITTA DOLENTE :  
> PER ME SI VA NELL’ ETERNO DOLORE :  
> PER ME SI VA TRA LA PERDUTA GENTE.  
> GIUSTIZIA MOSSE ‘L MIO ALTO FATTORE.  
> ……………………………………………….  
> LASCIATE OGNI SPERANZA, VOI CH’ ENTRATE.[^2]

This is the inscription from the gate of Hell in Dante's *Inferno* (Canto III), quoted verbatim in Italian to warn of the path into eternal suffering.[^3]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [Chapter 21](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/21-de-l-action-des-lois-de-la-morale-et-des-obstacles/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/21-de-l-action-des-lois-de-la-morale-et-des-obstacles/translation/#p-dbf447).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/21-de-l-action-des-lois-de-la-morale-et-des-obstacles/translation/#p-c8e46f).
